params.text:
Incidents, errors and near misses

However careful we are, things sometimes go wrong. An incident is an event that caused harm or could have caused harm. An error is a mistake, such as giving the wrong medication. A near miss is something that could have gone wrong but was caught in time and did not cause harm.

All three must be reported, even when no one was hurt. Near misses are especially valuable: they are early warnings that let a service fix a problem before someone comes to harm. Reporting them is never about blame — it is about learning and keeping people safe.

params.alt: Illustration for Incidents and near misses

params.text:
Duty of candour: being open and honest

The duty of candour is the legal requirement to be open, honest and transparent when something goes wrong. It applies to whole organisations and to every worker within them.

When something goes wrong that affects a person's care, you must:

  • tell the person (or their representative) what happened, in a way they can understand
  • offer a genuine, sincere apology — saying sorry is not an admission of legal liability
  • explain what will be done to put things right and to stop it happening again
  • report and record the event honestly and promptly

Covering up a mistake is far more serious than the mistake itself. Being open builds trust and allows the whole team to learn.

params.question: A near miss that caused no harm still needs to be reported.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Near misses must still be reported, even when no one was hurt, because they help the service learn and prevent future harm.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Near misses are early warnings. Reporting them lets a service learn and fix problems before anyone is harmed.
